body.value: A practical Terraform automation setup often separates reusable code from environment-specific configuration. One clean pattern is to keep a single root module and provide environment-specific backend settings and variable files externally.

value.caption: Example directory layout
value.code:
taco-wagon-app/
├─ backend/
│  ├─ dev.hcl
│  ├─ staging.hcl
│  ├─ prodeast.hcl
│  └─ prodwest.hcl
├─ environments/
│  ├─ dev.tfvars
│  ├─ staging.tfvars
│  ├─ prodeast.tfvars
│  └─ prodwest.tfvars
├─ .github/workflows/
│  ├─ ci.yml
│  ├─ cd.yml
│  ├─ release.yml
│  └─ deploy-environment.yml
├─ providers.tf
├─ versions.tf
├─ variables.tf
└─ outputs.tf

body.value: This pattern keeps the code reusable while allowing each environment to supply its own backend and variable values.

There are three broad Terraform workflows worth automating:

  • Code validation – formatting, syntax, linting, and documentation updates
  • Plan generation and review – producing readable plans for pull requests
  • Apply and promotion – deploying changes through dev, staging, and production

Code validation is best executed early and often. Plan and apply steps are usually safer in centralized automation platforms where access can be controlled and logs are preserved.

The first useful automation layer lives on the developer workstation. A pre-commit hook can run before Git creates a commit, preventing invalid or poorly formatted infrastructure code from being committed in the first place.

A common tool for this is pre-commit, which reads hook definitions from YAML and installs them into the repository as Git hooks.

value.caption: Local validation before code ever leaves the workstation
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[git commit] --> B[pre-commit hook]
B --> C[terraform fmt]
B --> D[terraform validate]
B --> E[tflint]
B --> F[terraform-docs]
C --> G{All checks pass?}
D --> G
E --> G
F --> G
G -->|Yes| H[Commit created]
G -->|No| I[Commit blocked]

value.caption: Typical local commands
value.code:
terraform init -backend=false
pre-commit run -a
pre-commit install
git add .
git commit -m "Add pre-commit hooks"

body.value: Using terraform init -backend=false is important when validation needs provider plugins but the remote backend is not configured yet.

value.title: Why this matters
value.text: The closer feedback is to the developer, the cheaper the fix is. Pre-commit hooks catch issues before code review and before CI time is wasted.

body.value:
Terraform automation gets stronger when you use specialized tools for each stage:

  • terraform fmt for formatting
  • terraform validate for syntax and internal consistency
  • TFLint for Terraform-specific linting and best practices
  • terraform-docs for generated documentation
  • Trivy, Checkov, or KICS for policy and security scanning
  • tf-summarize for human-friendly plan summaries

Together, these tools make CI feedback both stricter and easier to understand.

Continuous integration means that when a pull request is opened or updated, automation validates the code and generates plans that teammates can review. In Terraform, CI is less about compiling software and more about verifying configuration quality and showing proposed infrastructure changes.

value.caption: A Terraform-oriented CI workflow
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Pull request opened or updated] --> B[Format check]
A --> C[Validate]
A --> D[TFLint]
A --> E[Security scan]
B --> F[Plan job]
C --> F
D --> F
E --> F
F --> G[Store plan artifact]
F --> H[Post plan summary to PR]

value.caption: Typical CI environment variables for Terraform
value.code:
env:
  TF_LOG: INFO
  TF_INPUT: false
  TF_IN_AUTOMATION: true

body.value: These environment variables make Terraform friendlier for automation. TF_INPUT=false prevents interactive prompts. TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true reduces interactive-style output and signals that Terraform is running unattended.

value.caption: High-level CI job structure
value.code:
name: CI
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  format:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  lint:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  security:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  plan:
    needs: [format, validate, lint, security]
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

body.value: The checks can run in parallel for speed. The plan should wait until the earlier checks pass, because there is little value in generating a plan for code that already fails formatting, validation, linting, or security checks.

value.title: Why Save the Plan as an Artifact?

body.value:
Storing the plan file as a pipeline artifact gives later workflows a stable deployment input. This is useful when your delivery workflow wants to apply the exact plan reviewed during CI rather than recomputing a fresh plan later.

That said, this is also a design decision. Some teams prefer to re-plan during delivery to ensure freshness. Others prefer artifact promotion to preserve the exact reviewed output. The correct choice depends on your change control requirements and tolerance for drift between plan and apply.

value.title: Key trade-off
value.text: Applying a reviewed saved plan increases fidelity between review and deployment. Re-planning later increases freshness but may produce a different result than what reviewers approved.

body.value: Once a pull request is merged, a delivery workflow can deploy changes to lower environments such as development and staging. A common pattern is to retrieve the CI run identifier, download the saved plan artifact, initialize Terraform with the correct backend and environment values, and then apply the plan.

value.caption: Merge-based delivery to lower environments
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Pull request merged to main] --> B[Find related CI run]
B --> C[Download saved plan artifact]
C --> D[terraform init for dev]
D --> E[terraform apply plan in dev]
E --> F{Deployment successful?}
F -->|Yes| G[Deploy staging]
F -->|No| H[Stop pipeline and investigate]

body.value: This pattern provides controlled promotion. You can deploy environment by environment in sequence so that failures halt further promotion.

There is more than one way to trigger Terraform deployment:

  • After merge – simple and common
  • By pull request comment – deploy before merge for extra confidence
  • By release tag – especially useful for production

The course emphasizes that each option has trade-offs. Deploying after merge keeps the main workflow simple, but if deployment fails, broken code is already on the main branch. Deploying before merge keeps unstable code isolated to the feature branch, but the environment may temporarily diverge from main.

value.caption: Comparing deployment timing choices
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Feature branch] --> B[PR opened]
B --> C[Plan reviewed]
C --> D{Deployment trigger}
D -->|After merge| E[Merge to main first]
D -->|PR comment| F[Deploy from PR before merge]
D -->|Release tag| G[Deploy later from tagged version]

body.value:
Supporting multiple Terraform environments is easy in theory and difficult in practice. The course compares three common approaches:

  • Workspaces
  • Separate folders or directories
  • Pipeline-driven environments

Each approach solves the problem differently, and none is universally best.

value.title: Terraform Community Workspaces: Useful but Limited

body.value:
Terraform Community Edition workspaces let you reuse the same root module with separate instances of state. This can be useful for temporary environments, but there are important limitations for long-lived environments.

  • All workspaces share the same backend type and often the same broad access pattern
  • There is no strong built-in access control between environments
  • It is easy to apply the wrong code to the wrong workspace if your operational process is weak
  • Managing environment-specific variables through workspace-aware logic becomes hard to maintain at scale

They are convenient for testing, but not always ideal as the long-term answer for serious environment promotion.

value.text: A short-lived testing environment is a good fit for Community Edition workspaces. A tightly controlled promotion pipeline across dev, staging, and production usually needs more structure.

value.caption: Three common approaches to multi-environment Terraform
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Multi-environment Terraform] --> B[Separate folders]
A --> C[Long-lived branches]
A --> D[Pipeline-managed environments]
B --> B1[Clear separation but duplication risk]
C --> C1[Consistent root module but branch promotion complexity]
D --> D1[Single trunk with orchestrated promotion]

body.value: Folders make environments visible but can drift if files are copied inconsistently. Branches preserve a single root module per environment branch but add promotion complexity. Pipelines centralize orchestration and fit well with trunk-based development, though environments may temporarily run slightly different versions while promotion is in progress.

The pipeline-based model is a strong choice when you want one main branch and a clear promotion chain. A pull request generates plans for all target environments. After merge, lower environments are deployed in sequence. Once they are validated, a release tag triggers production deployment.

value.caption: Promotion path across environments
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[PR CI: plan dev, staging, prodeast, prodwest] --> B[Merge to main]
B --> C[Deploy dev]
C --> D[Validate dev]
D --> E[Deploy staging]
E --> F[Validate staging]
F --> G[Create release tag]
G --> H[Deploy prodeast]
G --> I[Deploy prodwest]

body.value: This approach keeps short-lived feature branches and uses the pipeline itself to manage ordering, promotion, and safety boundaries.

GitHub Actions matrix jobs are a convenient way to generate plans for multiple environments without duplicating job definitions. Each matrix entry can select the correct backend file, tfvars file, and artifact name for its environment.

value.caption: Conceptual matrix strategy
value.code:
strategy:
  fail-fast: false
  matrix:
    environment: [dev, staging, prodeast, prodwest]

body.value: Turning off fail-fast ensures that even if one environment plan fails, the others can still complete and provide useful information for reviewers.

Production deployments deserve stronger control. A common pattern is to promote through dev and staging first, and only then create a release tag that triggers the production deployment pipeline. That makes production deployment intentional and traceable.

value.caption: Release-triggered production rollout
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Lower environments validated] --> B[Create version tag, for example v1.0.0]
B --> C[Release pipeline starts]
C --> D[Find reviewed plan artifacts]
D --> E[Deploy prodeast]
D --> F[Deploy prodwest]
E --> G[Production verified]
F --> G

body.value: When infrastructure spans regions, production deployment may run in parallel or serially depending on risk tolerance, platform limits, and rollback strategy.

A strong automation pipeline also depends on operational details that are easy to overlook:

  • Use .gitignore to exclude .terraform, state files, and sensitive files
  • Consider checking in .terraform.lock.hcl for provider version consistency
  • Use plugin caching where available to speed up repeated runs
  • Provide credentials securely through pipeline secrets, machine identity, or dynamic credentials
  • Decide whether to stop immediately on failure or allow broader diagnostics first
  • Think carefully about logging, retry behavior, and auditability

value.caption: Common Terraform automation environment variables
value.code:
export TF_IN_AUTOMATION=true
export TF_INPUT=false
export TF_LOG=INFO
export TF_LOG_PATH=terraform.log
export TF_CLI_ARGS="-no-color"

body.value: These variables help make Terraform more predictable in non-interactive environments.

value.title: Manual Deploy and Manual Destroy Workflows

body.value:
Even with strong CI/CD, teams often need operational escape hatches. Manual deployment workflows can be useful when a deployment must be retried for a single environment. Manual destroy workflows help clean up dev, staging, or full stacks after testing.

These workflows should be treated as controlled operational tools, not the default delivery path.

value.title: HCP Terraform: Why Use a Dedicated IaC Platform?

body.value: GitHub Actions can automate Terraform very effectively, but it also means you build and maintain much of the process yourself. A platform such as HCP Terraform provides state management, workspace-level execution, run history, policy checks, variable storage, access control, and VCS integration out of the box.

value.caption: What HCP Terraform adds on top of Terraform Community Edition
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Terraform code] --> B[HCP Terraform workspace]
B --> C[Remote state]
B --> D[Run history]
B --> E[Variable management]
B --> F[Policy checks]
B --> G[Access control]
B --> H[VCS-triggered runs]

body.value: This reduces the amount of custom pipeline logic you need to own and maintain.

In HCP Terraform, a workspace represents a combination of Terraform code, state, settings, variables, and automation behavior. Workspaces belong to projects, and projects belong to organizations. Settings such as credentials, variables, and policies can be inherited from higher levels.

HCP Terraform supports different workflows, including CLI-driven, VCS-driven, and API-driven runs. For GitOps-style infrastructure automation, the VCS workflow is usually the most natural fit.

value.caption: HCP Terraform hierarchy
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Organization] --> B[Project]
B --> C[Workspace: dev]
B --> D[Workspace: staging]
B --> E[Workspace: prodeast]
B --> F[Workspace: prodwest]

value.title: Migrating from GitHub Actions to HCP Terraform

body.value:
A practical migration path is not to replace everything at once. Keep local pre-commit hooks. Keep lightweight CI checks such as format, validate, lint, and security scanning in GitHub Actions. Then move planning and deployment responsibilities to HCP Terraform workspaces.

With VCS integration, pull requests can trigger speculative runs. Merges can trigger standard runs for lower environments. Tags can trigger production runs. Manual deploy and destroy workflows can often be retired because the platform already supports them.

value.caption: Hybrid model during migration
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Developer commits code] --> B[Pre-commit hooks]
B --> C[GitHub PR]
C --> D[GitHub Actions validation]
C --> E[HCP Terraform speculative plan]
D --> F[Merge approved]
E --> F
F --> G[HCP Terraform dev run]
G --> H[HCP Terraform staging run]
H --> I[Release tag]
I --> J[HCP Terraform production runs]

body.value: One of the valuable platform-level additions is policy as code. In the course example, a Sentinel policy verifies that VPC resources include required tags such as owner and environment. This allows governance rules to be enforced consistently during runs.

value.caption: Example idea behind an infrastructure policy
value.code:
Policy goal:
- Every VPC must include an owner tag
- Every VPC must include an environment tag
- Violations are reported during the run
- Enforcement can start as advisory before becoming mandatory

value.text: Starting policy checks in advisory mode is a smart way to introduce governance without breaking every existing workflow on day one.

body.value: To see the whole model in action, imagine adding a default owner tag in the AWS provider configuration. The developer updates code locally, pre-commit runs, a pull request opens, CI validates the code, plans are reviewed, lower environments are deployed after merge, and production is promoted through a release.

value.caption: End-to-end GitOps promotion for a Terraform change
value.diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant Git as GitHub
    participant CI as CI Pipeline
    participant CD as Delivery Pipeline
    participant Prod as Release Pipeline

    Dev->>Git: Push feature branch
    Git->>CI: Trigger validation and plans
    CI-->>Git: Post plan summaries to PR
    Dev->>Git: Merge pull request
    Git->>CD: Trigger dev deployment
    CD->>CD: Validate and deploy staging
    Dev->>Git: Create release tag
    Git->>Prod: Trigger production deployment

body.value:
• Automate validation locally first, then centrally
  • Use pull requests to review Terraform plans, not just code diffs
  • Treat environments as a promotion pipeline, not isolated manual targets
  • Be deliberate about when deployment happens: after merge, before merge, or by release
  • Prefer simple, visible patterns over clever but fragile ones
  • Use a dedicated IaC platform when you need stronger state management, policy, access control, and run orchestration

The most important idea is that Terraform automation is not one specific toolchain. It is a design problem. Your pipeline should reflect your team structure, your compliance requirements, your risk tolerance, and the number of environments you manage.

value.title: Final takeaway
value.text: The best Terraform automation solution is the one that makes infrastructure changes reviewable, repeatable, and safe enough for your real operating model.

title: Terraform Resource Lifecycle Management Explained
excerpt: Learn how Terraform manages resource creation, updates, replacement, imports, moves, and removals. This standalone guide explains the resource graph, depends_on, lifecycle rules, terraform_data, query, import, moved, and removed blocks with practical examples.

body.value:
Terraform is not only a provisioning tool. Its real strength is that it manages infrastructure across the full resource lifecycle: create, update, replace, and destroy.

In real environments, infrastructure is rarely static. Resources are renamed, upgraded, imported from manual deployments, split across modules, or removed from management without being destroyed. Lifecycle management is the part of Terraform that lets you handle those changes safely and deliberately.

value.title: Core idea
value.text: Terraform is constantly comparing configuration, state, and real infrastructure. Lifecycle features let you control how Terraform reacts when those three no longer line up perfectly.

value.caption: The main lifecycle phases of a Terraform-managed resource
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Create] --> B[Update in place]
B --> C{Change requires replacement?}
C -->|No| B
C -->|Yes| D[Replace]
D --> E[Destroy old resource]
B --> F[Retire from management or destroy]

body.value:
At the simplest level, every managed resource follows the same broad lifecycle:

  • Create – Terraform provisions the resource for the first time
  • Update – Terraform changes mutable attributes in place
  • Replace – Terraform destroys and recreates a resource when an immutable attribute changes
  • Destroy – Terraform removes the resource entirely

In practice, Terraform also supports several advanced lifecycle operations:

  • Importing an existing resource into state
  • Moving a resource to a new address
  • Migrating resources between configurations
  • Stopping management of a resource without destroying it

value.title: How Terraform Decides What to Do: The Resource Graph

body.value:
When you run terraform plan, Terraform builds an acyclic dependency graph. Each managed object becomes a node, and references between objects become edges in that graph.

This graph is what Terraform uses to determine:

  • which resources depend on others
  • what can be created in parallel
  • what must happen first
  • what actions are required to reach the desired state

value.caption: Example dependency graph: a VPC and two subnets
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
VPC[aws_vpc.main]
SUB1[aws_subnet.public_a]
SUB2[aws_subnet.public_b]
SUB1 --> VPC
SUB2 --> VPC

body.value:
In this example, both subnets reference the VPC ID. Because the VPC ID does not exist until the VPC is created, Terraform knows it must create the VPC first. After that, it can create both subnets in parallel.

This is one of Terraform’s most important behaviors: references imply dependencies.

value.caption: Generate and inspect the dependency graph
value.code:
terraform graph
terraform graph -type=plan
terraform plan -out=tfplan
terraform graph tfplan

value.title: Practical tip
value.text: The raw output of terraform graph is Graphviz dot text. It becomes much easier to understand when pasted into a Graphviz visualizer.

body.value:
Most dependencies in Terraform should be expressed through direct references. For example, if an EC2 instance uses a subnet ID, that reference already tells Terraform about the dependency.

Sometimes, though, you need to enforce ordering even when one resource does not directly reference another. That is when you use depends_on.

value.caption: Explicit dependency with depends_on

body.value:
This tells Terraform to complete all actions on the logging bucket before it acts on the EC2 instance.

Use depends_on carefully. It is helpful for special cases, but explicit references are usually better because they express the real relationship more precisely.

value.title: The lifecycle Block: Fine-Grained Control Over Resource Behavior

body.value: The lifecycle block is Terraform’s main mechanism for altering default resource behavior. It is supported by managed resources and allows you to influence creation, replacement, destruction, and drift handling.

value.caption: Lifecycle block structure

body.value:
The most important lifecycle arguments covered in this course are:

  • create_before_destroy
  • prevent_destroy
  • ignore_changes
  • replace_triggered_by

Together, they let you change how Terraform handles replacement and drift.

value.title: create_before_destroy: Reverse the Default Replacement Order

body.value:
By default, when Terraform must replace a resource, it typically destroys the old object first and then creates the new one. That is often represented in the plan as -/+.

Some resources cannot be destroyed while they are still attached or in use. A security group attached to a running EC2 instance is a classic example. In such cases, destroying first will fail.

value.caption: Default replacement vs create_before_destroy
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Default replacement] --> B[Destroy old resource]
B --> C[Create new resource]
D[With create_before_destroy] --> E[Create new resource]
E --> F[Switch dependencies]
F --> G[Destroy old resource]

value.caption: Create the replacement before destroying the old object

body.value:
This is especially useful when a platform requires the old resource to remain available until references have been switched to the new one.

There are two important caveats:

  • Some platforms require globally unique names, so creating the replacement first may fail unless the new resource uses a different name or identifier
  • Terraform may mark the old resource as deposed in state and attempt to delete it later if deletion fails during the same apply

value.title: replace_triggered_by: Replace a Resource When Another Thing Changes

body.value:
Sometimes a resource should be replaced when something related changes, even though Terraform would not normally infer that relationship. For example, you might want to recreate an S3 cache bucket when a new application version is released.

That is what replace_triggered_by is for.

value.caption: Using terraform_data to trigger replacement from an arbitrary value

body.value: This pattern works because replace_triggered_by can only reference managed resources, not variables, locals, or data sources directly. The built-in terraform_data resource acts as a bridge between an arbitrary value and Terraform’s replacement engine.

value.caption: How terraform_data enables value-based replacement
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[application_version variable] --> B[terraform_data.application_version]
B --> C[replace_triggered_by]
C --> D[Recreate asset cache bucket]

body.value:
Some resources are too important to delete casually. Databases, audit logs, long-lived buckets, and compliance-related artifacts often fall into this category.

Terraform provides a guardrail through prevent_destroy.

value.caption: Protect a resource from recreation or destruction

body.value:
When prevent_destroy is enabled, Terraform will block any plan that would destroy or replace that resource. In-place updates are still allowed.

This is not a substitute for platform-level protection such as IAM controls or deletion locks, but it is a valuable extra safeguard inside the Terraform workflow.

value.title: Important limitation
value.text: Anyone who can edit the Terraform configuration can remove or disable prevent_destroy. Treat it as a deliberate guardrail, not an absolute protection mechanism.

value.title: ignore_changes: Let Another System Manage Selected Attributes

body.value:
Sometimes Terraform creates a resource, but another system is responsible for maintaining part of it afterward. Common examples include tags updated by organization policies, settings enforced by Kubernetes controllers, or changes made by Ansible.

If Terraform keeps trying to revert those external changes, you get a tug-of-war between systems. ignore_changes solves that problem.

value.caption: Ignore changes to externally managed tags

body.value:
Terraform still uses the configured value during creation, but later plans will ignore differences for the listed attributes.

Two subtle points matter here:

  • If the resource is recreated, Terraform uses the values from configuration again
  • Even ignored attributes can still be refreshed into state during apply or refresh-only operations
Sometimes you know a resource is unhealthy even though Terraform does not detect any configuration drift. In that situation, you can explicitly force replacement during planning or apply.

value.caption: Force replacement of a single resource
value.code:
terraform plan  -replace=aws_instance.app
terraform apply -replace=aws_instance.app

body.value: This is useful when the real infrastructure is broken in a way Terraform cannot infer from configuration or state alone.

Terraform is often adopted after infrastructure already exists. In that case, you need to bring existing resources under management without destroying and recreating them.

The course covers two import styles:

  • Imperative import using terraform import
  • Declarative import using import blocks

value.caption: Two import approaches
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Existing resource in cloud] --> B{Import approach}
B --> C[terraform import command]
B --> D[import block + plan + apply]
C --> E[State updated directly]
D --> F[Reviewed through execution plan]

body.value: The older terraform import command updates state directly and handles one resource at a time. The newer declarative import block fits Terraform’s normal review workflow much better.

value.caption: Older imperative import command
value.code: terraform import aws_vpc.main vpc-1234567890abcdef0

value.caption: Preferred declarative import block

body.value: With import blocks, Terraform can show exactly what will be imported during terraform plan. It can even attempt to generate configuration using -generate-config-out, although the generated blocks are often verbose and require cleanup.

value.title: Discovering Existing Resources with terraform query

body.value:
One of the hardest parts of importing is discovering the identifiers of unmanaged resources. Terraform 1.14 introduces a native answer: terraform query.

The query workflow uses files ending in .tfquery.hcl and a special list block for provider-supported resource discovery.

value.caption: A simple query file for EC2 discovery
Run a Terraform query
value.code:
terraform query
terraform query -json

body.value: This is especially helpful when teams have created resources outside Terraform and you need a Terraform-native way to discover what is already there.

Refactoring Terraform code should not force infrastructure recreation. If a resource is renamed or moved into a module, its address changes. Without guidance, Terraform assumes the old resource disappeared and a new one should be created.

The moved block lets you declare that the resource address changed, but the real object did not.

value.caption: Move a resource to a new address
Move a root resource into a child module
What moved blocks really do
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Old address in state] --> B[moved block]
B --> C[New address in state]
C --> D[No infrastructure recreation]

body.value: Moved blocks are ideal when refactoring within a root module or inside a reusable module. They preserve infrastructure while aligning state with the new code structure.

Moving resources to an entirely different root module is more involved. The clean declarative approach is:

  1. Add import blocks and resource blocks to the destination configuration
  2. Apply the destination configuration so it starts managing the resources
  3. Add removed blocks to the source configuration
  4. Apply the source configuration so those resources are removed from state without being destroyed

value.caption: Remove a resource from Terraform management without destroying it
Declarative migration between configurations
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Source config manages resource] --> B[Destination imports resource]
B --> C[Both configs temporarily know about it]
C --> D[Source uses removed block]
D --> E[Source state forgets resource]
E --> F[Destination remains sole manager]

body.value: This approach avoids direct state surgery and keeps the migration visible in code and in execution plans.

value.title: Why this matters
value.text: Compared with imperative state commands, the combination of import and removed blocks is safer, reviewable, repeatable, and easier for teams to understand later.

value.title: Imperative State Commands vs Declarative Workflow

body.value:
The course also covers older state manipulation commands such as terraform state mv and terraform state rm. These commands still exist and can be useful in edge cases, especially with local state, but they operate directly on state outside the normal plan and apply workflow.

That is the main reason the declarative model is preferred whenever possible.

value.caption: Examples of imperative state commands
value.code:
terraform state mv aws_instance.web module.app.aws_instance.web
terraform state rm aws_instance.web

body.value: Use these only when you fully understand the consequences. In team environments, keeping lifecycle changes in code is usually the better long-term choice.

value.title: A Practical Mental Model for Lifecycle Decisions

value.caption: Choosing the right Terraform lifecycle feature

body.value: If you keep this decision tree in mind, most lifecycle scenarios become much easier to reason about.

• Terraform builds a dependency graph from references and uses it to determine order of operations
  • depends_on is for rare cases where you must declare a dependency explicitly
  • create_before_destroy helps when destroy-first replacement would fail
  • replace_triggered_by lets one resource force replacement of another
  • prevent_destroy adds a guardrail against accidental destruction
  • ignore_changes prevents drift battles with other systems
  • import, terraform query, moved, and removed are essential for adopting Terraform safely in real-world environments

Resource lifecycle management is where Terraform becomes much more than a simple provisioning tool. It becomes an operational framework for evolving infrastructure safely over time.

title: Terraform: Managing Multiple Configurations — Data Sources, Remote State, and Cross-Configuration Sharing
excerpt: Learn how Terraform data sources work, how to split a large configuration into smaller ones, when to use terraform_remote_state, why teams move to SSM Parameter Store or other external stores, and how to manage cross-configuration dependencies safely.

value.title: Why Managing Multiple Terraform Configurations Matters

body.value:
When teams first adopt Terraform, they often begin with a single configuration that keeps growing over time. At first this is convenient, but eventually it becomes harder to maintain, slower to evolve, and more difficult to divide across teams.

That is where multiple related configurations come in. Instead of one large Terraform setup, you split infrastructure into smaller units such as networking, application, IAM, or database layers. The challenge is that once you split configurations, you still need a reliable way to pass information between them.

This article explains the key techniques for doing exactly that: data sources, remote state, and external shared stores such as AWS SSM Parameter Store.

value.title: Core idea
value.text: Splitting Terraform into multiple configurations improves ownership and maintainability, but it also creates a new problem: configurations still need to exchange information safely.

value.caption: How a monolithic Terraform setup evolves into multiple configurations
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Single Terraform configuration] --> B[Growth in size and complexity]
B --> C[Split by concerns or teams]
C --> D[Networking config]
C --> E[App config]
C --> F[Database config]
C --> G[IAM config]
D --> H[Need to share data]
E --> H
F --> H
G --> H

value.title: What Is a Terraform Data Source?

body.value:
A data source in Terraform is a provider-defined object used to query information from an external platform or service. Unlike a resource, a data source does not create or modify infrastructure. It simply retrieves information that your configuration can use.

You can think of a data source as a read-only resource. It has a block syntax similar to resources, and its attributes are available for use elsewhere in the configuration.

Terraform refreshes data sources while building an execution plan, which means their values can change over time depending on what they query.

value.text: Resources create or manage infrastructure. Data sources read or generate information for use in your configuration.

value.caption: Resources vs data sources
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Terraform configuration] --> B[Resources]
A --> C[Data sources]
B --> D[Create / update infrastructure]
C --> E[Read or generate information]

value.title: Ways to Bring External Information into Terraform

body.value:
Terraform configurations commonly use three approaches when they need information from outside the current code:

  • Input variables – values supplied at runtime
  • File functions – values loaded from files or templates
  • Data sources – values queried from providers or generated by special data source types

All three are useful, but data sources are especially powerful because they can make your configuration more dynamic and less dependent on hardcoded values.

Data sources are ideal when your configuration should discover information instead of requiring someone to type it manually. Typical use cases include:

  • Querying the list of AWS availability zones in the current region
  • Finding the latest AMI that matches a filter
  • Looking up an existing VPC and subnets for an application deployment
  • Reading shared configuration values from another service

Whenever Terraform needs to use information that already exists somewhere else, data sources are often the right tool.

value.caption: Examples of dynamic lookups with data sources
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Terraform config] --> B[Availability zones]
A --> C[Latest AMI]
A --> D[Existing VPC]
A --> E[Shared configuration values]

body.value: The syntax of a data source closely resembles a resource block. The main difference is the leading data keyword.

value.caption: General data source syntax

body.value: Data sources also support common meta-arguments such as count, for_each, provider, and depends_on. Some support lifecycle precondition and postcondition blocks as well.
When you reference a data source, the identifier begins with data. This is easy to forget if you are used to referencing resources directly.

value.caption: Reference pattern
value.code: data.<data_source_type>.<name_label>.<attribute>

value.caption: Example: VPC IDs from aws_vpcs
value.code: data.aws_vpcs.app_net.ids

value.text: If Terraform says there is no matching resource for a reference, check whether you forgot to prefix it with data.

value.title: A Real Example: Making a Terraform App More Dynamic

body.value:
In the course scenario, a Taco Wagon application deploys a VPC, two public subnets, a security group, and an EC2 instance. The original configuration relied on input variables for:

  • Availability zones
  • AMI ID
  • A startup script embedded directly as a heredoc

That worked, but it was too manual. It required users to look up AZs and AMIs ahead of time and paste them into terraform.tfvars. This is exactly the kind of friction data sources are meant to reduce.

value.caption: Original deployment model
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[terraform.tfvars] --> B[Availability zones]
A --> C[AMI ID]
A --> D[Other variables]
B --> E[Subnets]
C --> F[EC2 instance]
D --> E
D --> F

value.title: Replacing Availability Zone Input with a Data Source

body.value: Instead of manually passing AZ names, the configuration can use aws_availability_zones to query the currently available zones in the active AWS region. This reduces manual work and makes the configuration more portable.

value.caption: Availability zones data source

body.value: This changes the design from “tell Terraform exactly which zones to use” to “tell Terraform how many zones to use, and let it discover them.” That is a much better level of abstraction.

value.title: Replacing a Hardcoded AMI with a Data Source

body.value: Hardcoding an AMI ID is fragile because AMI IDs differ by region and become outdated over time. Terraform can query the most recent matching image using the aws_ami data source.

value.caption: Finding the latest Amazon Linux 2 AMI

value.text: Using a data source for AMIs makes the configuration far easier to reuse across regions and helps avoid stale image IDs.

body.value: After adding data sources, Terraform will load them during planning and refresh operations. You can inspect values in several ways, including terraform console.

value.caption: Inspecting a data source
value.code:
terraform console
> data.aws_availability_zones.available.names

body.value: This is a simple but very useful debugging technique whenever you want to verify exactly what a data source is returning.

Not all data sources read from remote platforms. Some are used to generate structured documents inside Terraform. Two important examples are:

  • aws_iam_policy_document – builds IAM policy JSON
  • cloudinit_config – builds cloud-init or startup content for instances

These data sources are not querying a provider for existing objects. Instead, they help Terraform produce correctly formatted output that other resources can consume.

value.caption: Two categories of data sources
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Data sources] --> B[Query external systems]
A --> C[Generate structured documents]
B --> D[aws_availability_zones]
B --> E[aws_ami]
C --> F[aws_iam_policy_document]
C --> G[cloudinit_config]

value.title: Replacing Heredoc Startup Scripts with cloudinit_config

body.value: A heredoc string embedded directly inside a resource works for a quick demo, but it becomes hard to maintain as startup logic grows. A better approach is to store script content in a template file and use the cloudinit_config data source to generate the final payload.

value.caption: cloudinit_config with a templated shell script

body.value: This approach scales better because the script can live in its own file, accept variables through templatefile(), and later grow into multiple cloud-init parts if needed.

As infrastructure grows, it often stops making sense to manage everything from one root module. Teams may split by ownership, responsibility, deployment cadence, or blast radius.

For example, one team may manage networking, another IAM, another databases, and application teams may own only their application stack.

value.caption: From one terralith to team-owned configurations
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Monolithic Terraform config] --> B[Networking config]
A --> C[Database config]
A --> D[IAM config]
A --> E[Application config]
B --> F[Network team]
C --> G[DB team]
D --> H[Security team]
E --> I[Application team]

value.title: Important trade-off
value.text: Splitting infrastructure improves maintainability and ownership, but it removes Terraform’s single global dependency graph.

value.title: Sharing Data Across Configurations with terraform_remote_state

body.value:
Inside one configuration, Terraform can pass data directly using resource references, module inputs, and outputs. Across separate configurations, you need a different mechanism. One option is terraform_remote_state.

This data source allows one configuration to read the root module outputs of another configuration’s state file.

value.caption: terraform_remote_state example

body.value: If the source configuration publishes vpc_id and public_subnet_ids as outputs, another configuration can consume them. This is conceptually similar to how parent and child modules exchange outputs and inputs.

value.title: Moving State to S3 for Separate Configurations

body.value:
In the course scenario, the Taco Wagon team split their infrastructure into two configurations:

  • Network – VPC, subnets, and related networking components
  • App – security group and EC2 instance

Because the configurations now live independently, each uses its own state backend in S3. The app configuration then reads outputs from the network configuration through terraform_remote_state.

value.caption: Remote state sharing between split configurations
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Network config] --> B[S3 state backend]
B --> C[terraform_remote_state]
C --> D[App config]
A --> E[Outputs: vpc_id, public_subnet_ids]

value.title: The Two Big Drawbacks of terraform_remote_state

body.value:
The course highlights two major concerns with using terraform_remote_state as a long-term integration mechanism.

  1. Security – the caller needs read access to the entire state object, not just outputs
  2. Tight coupling – downstream configurations depend directly on the state backend shape and output names of the source configuration

If the source configuration changes its backend, output names, or output types, dependent configurations may break.

value.title: Why this matters
value.text: Even if a downstream config only reads outputs, the credentials behind terraform_remote_state typically need access to the full state file, which may contain sensitive data.

value.caption: Why remote state can become risky
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Source config state] --> B[Outputs]
A --> C[All other state data]
D[Downstream config] --> E[Read access required]
E --> A
C --> F[Potential exposure]
B --> G[Needed values]

value.title: A Better Security Option in HCP Terraform: tfe_outputs

body.value:
If your organization uses HCP Terraform or Terraform Enterprise, the course recommends considering tfe_outputs instead of terraform_remote_state. This data source queries outputs through the API rather than direct state access.

That gives two improvements:

  • the calling configuration can be granted access only to outputs
  • sensitive outputs are handled more intentionally

This does not eliminate logical coupling between configurations, but it improves the security model significantly.

value.caption: tfe_outputs example

value.title: External Shared Stores: Decoupling Configurations Properly

body.value:
If you want to reduce both security concerns and tight coupling, a stronger pattern is to publish shared configuration data to an external service instead of directly exposing Terraform state.

The course mentions several categories:

  • Key-value stores such as AWS SSM Parameter Store
  • Configuration stores such as Azure App Configuration
  • Secrets systems such as AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault
  • DNS for names and addresses
  • Other stores such as Redis, Consul, or Kubernetes ConfigMaps

In this model, the consuming configuration only cares that the data is published in the expected location. It no longer cares how the producing team internally manages its Terraform state.

value.caption: Decoupled sharing through an external system
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
A[Network config] --> B[Publish values]
B --> C[AWS SSM Parameter Store]
C --> D[App config data source]
D --> E[Use vpc_id and subnet IDs]

value.title: Migrating from Remote State to AWS SSM Parameter Store

body.value:
In the final stage of the scenario, the networking team stops exposing outputs through remote state and instead writes shared values into AWS SSM Parameter Store. The application team then reads those values with data sources.

This removes direct dependence on the network state backend and gives more control over exactly what is shared.

value.caption: Publishing network data to SSM Parameter Store

body.value: Notice that StringList values still need to be supplied as strings to the API. That is why the subnet ID list is joined into a comma-separated string before being written.

value.title: Reading Shared SSM Parameters from the App Configuration

body.value: The application configuration can then read the published parameters using aws_ssm_parameter data sources. In the course, a for_each pattern is used so multiple parameters can be queried consistently.

value.caption: Reading network values from SSM

value.title: Why this is better
value.text: The app configuration now depends on published network values in SSM, not on the implementation details of the network team’s Terraform state.

body.value:
The course also mentions a few flexible or fallback-style data sources that can help when no first-class provider exists for your integration target:

  • local_file / local_sensitive_file – read from files
  • http – query an HTTP endpoint
  • external – run a program that accepts JSON on stdin and returns JSON on stdout

The external data source is especially flexible, but it creates a dependency on an external script or executable, so it should usually be treated as a last resort rather than a first choice.

Once infrastructure is split into multiple configurations, Terraform loses visibility into dependencies outside the currently running root module. That means a change in one configuration may require changes in another, but Terraform will not automatically know that.

Examples include:

  • Replacing subnets used by an app configuration
  • Renaming a resource that another configuration references indirectly
  • Moving resources to another configuration without updating consumers

This is one of the biggest operational differences between a single terralith and a multi-configuration environment.

value.caption: Dependency visibility before and after splitting configurations
value.diagram:
flowchart TD
A[Single configuration] --> B[One dependency graph]
C[Split configurations] --> D[Network graph]
C --> E[App graph]
C --> F[DB graph]
D -. hidden dependency .-> E
D -. hidden dependency .-> F

value.title: Practical Advice for Managing Multiple Terraform Configurations

body.value:
The course ends with practical guidance that is worth treating as best practice:

  • Minimize dependencies split across configurations whenever possible
  • Document cross-configuration dependencies clearly in README files
  • Trigger downstream plans when source configurations are applied successfully
  • Consider orchestration tools such as HCP Terraform Stacks, Terragrunt, or Terramate

Splitting Terraform is not just a code decision. It is also a team process and operational design decision.

value.title: Final takeaway
value.text: The right sharing mechanism depends on your organization. terraform_remote_state is simple, tfe_outputs improves security in HCP Terraform, and external shared stores such as SSM Parameter Store reduce tight coupling.

body.value:
Managing multiple Terraform configurations is really about balancing modularity, safety, and team ownership. Data sources help Terraform discover external information dynamically. Generative data sources help produce structured content like policies and startup payloads. Remote state can connect configurations quickly, but it introduces security and coupling trade-offs. External systems such as AWS SSM Parameter Store often provide a cleaner long-term integration pattern.

If you understand those trade-offs, you can design Terraform configurations that are not only technically correct, but also practical for real organizations and real teams.
